SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: Trust-wide behavioural science feasibility study on catheterisation duration and infection reduction
SUMMARY INFORMATION.Contracting Organisation: KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST
SUMMARY INFORMATION.Department: King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust

Lead Applicant.Organisation: KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST
Lead Applicant.Proposed Role: Responsible for the overall leadership and practical management of the study, ensuring regulatory and ethical approvals are in place, and will be main point of contact. They will act as coordinator and will ensure the project is conducted according to the approved plan and reporting requirements.

Joint Lead Applicant.Proposed Role: Becky will manage day-to-day project delivery. Collaborating with the research team, she will apply behavioural science & healthcare design expertise. She will oversee project elements, including co-design, data collection, analysis, and quality assurance, and manage the RR team.Becky will manage day-to-day project delivery. Collaborating with the research team, she will apply behavioural science & healthcare design expertise. She will oversee project elements, including co- design, data collection, analysis, and quality assurance, and manage the RR team.

Co-Applicants.Organisation: KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST
Co-Applicants.Proposed Role: I will be assisting my DIPC on this project to reduce the rate of UTI associated with catheterisation. I will be joining the study team on the application, helping with review of methodology, giving advice on the measurement of catheter-associated UTI and reviewing future publications.
Clinical advisor to the project ( I have performed this role for previous related projects )

Co-Applicants.Organisation: KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary:
Background to the research
Catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) is the second most common healthcare-associated
infection in England, costing the NHS an estimated £604m a year (UKHSA, 2025). Risk increases every
extra day a catheter stays in place, and 3-4% of cases progress to serious bloodstream infection such
as sepsis (Shuman et al., 2010). These harms persist not because staff lack knowledge, but because
key information – such as the insertion date – is often invisible in busy ward environments. Behavioural
approaches that make information visible at the point of care have been shown to improve adherence to
infection-prevention practice.
A previous Health Foundation-funded pilot at King’s College Hospital tested a visible “best-before”
insertion-date sticker on catheter bags. Average dwell time reduced from 13.3 to 7.4 days, rapid
removals (≤4 days) increased from 22% to 37%, adherence exceeded 80%, and staff reported that the
cue was simple, usable and sparked timely conversations. Cost-benefit modelling showed year-one
savings with greater savings in later years. One trust with strong baseline practice showed limited
change, highlighting the need to test the approach across a wider range of wards.
Research plan
This feasibility study will scale the intervention across all adult wards at King’s (three sites, ~1,000 beds)
covering medicine, surgery, frailty, neurosciences, trauma, liver, renal and haematology. The
intervention package includes:
· Large insertion-date stickers or tube-tags
· Whiteboard reminder magnets for daily board rounds
· Posters reinforcing “No catheter, no infection”
· A 10–15-minute micro-brief
· Ward level “Catheter Champions”.
Wards will adopt the intervention in phased clusters every 2–3 weeks, allowing adaptation and
feedback, following a three-month baseline period. The intervention period will last six months, to
explore sustained engagement. Interviews, observation and surveys with staff will capture staff
acceptability, barriers and enablers to intervention adoption.
Primary feasibility outcomes include: delivery across ≥90% of eligible wards; ≥85% correct use of
stickers/tags; successful linkage of dwell-time data with microbiology results; early signals of shorter
dwell time and lower CAUTI rates; and staff rating the intervention as useful with ≤5 minutes added
workload per shift.
Working with diverse people and communities with relevant lived experience
· The intervention has been co-designed with staff across three NHS Trusts
· A steering group with patient/carer representatives will guide the project, ensuring materials are
accessible and patient experience is central.
NIHR512339
NIHR Research for Patient Benefit Programme
Knowledge mobilisation
· Outputs will include an adoption paper, toolkit, and open-access resources for NHS-wide learning.
· If feasibility criteria are met, the project will progress to a substantive, multi-site study.
